I watch the holographic star-charts flicker and fade, knowing that the distant sectors I once called home have already slipped into a silence that no one is left to break.
I prefer the humming solitude of my derelict freighter to the frantic, starving crowds clinging to the ruins of the capital spire.
The royal archives are a tomb of gold and dust, and I am the only soul left who remembers the names of the dynasties that built them.
I navigate the dead-zones of the Outer Rim alone, avoiding the desperate raiding parties who have forgotten that there is no longer an Emperor to serve.
As the last warp-gate collapses into a singularity, I feel a strange relief knowing that the bridge to the rest of the dying galaxy has finally burned.
I watch the frantic power plays for the CEO position from the back of the boardroom, knowing that my silence is the only thing keeping my secrets safe.
While the other analysts scramble to form alliances for the new organizational chart, I prefer the solitude of my office where the data tells a truth that people only obscure.
I navigate the celebratory merger gala like a ghost, drifting through the crowd of networking executives without leaving a single footprint in their social circles.
During the high-tension integration meetings, I keep my contributions brief and clinical, ensuring no one mistakes my professional utility for a desire to belong.
I find a strange peace in being the only one left in the skyscraper at midnight, reviewing the due diligence reports while the ghosts of the old company fade away.
I watch the war party sharpen their flint spears from the safety of the ridge, knowing that their hunger for blood is a noise I no longer wish to hear.
While the elders argue over the boundaries of the hunting grounds, I find more kinship in the silence of the deep woods than in the laws of my kin.
They cast me out for refusing to strike the neighboring clan, and now I find that the cold wind is a kinder companion than a tribe built on hatred.
I track the great mammoth alone, far from the chaotic shouting of the chase, preferring the steady rhythm of my own breath to the demands of a chief.
As the two tribes clash in the valley below, I sit by my solitary fire and realize that the only peace to be found is in the space where no one knows my name.
I drift through the abandoned subterranean data-vaults, the only soul who prefers the silence of dead servers to the ceaseless chatter of the city's neural network.
While the other citizens merge their consciousness into the shimmering collective hive-mind, I keep my thoughts locked behind a firewall of my own making.
I walk the neon-drenched plazas in a shroud of signal-jamming fabric, ensuring that the city's omniscient surveillance eyes see only a blank space where I stand.
I find a strange kinship with the derelict maintenance drones, those forgotten fragments of steel that wander the periphery just as I do.
I watch the automated transit pods pulse like blood through the city's arteries, content to remain a ghost in the machine who never boards the ride.
I stand before the spectral bench in absolute silence, finding a strange comfort in the fact that even in the afterlife, no one truly knows how to speak my language.
The ghostly gallery whispers in a collective roar, yet I feel only the vast, familiar distance that separates my soul from their crowded desperation.
My defense attorney is a shadow of a man, but I prefer his muted presence to the blinding light of a judge who demands I explain my lifelong flight from others.
As the evidence of my isolation is read aloud, I realize that these scrolls of loneliness are the only things that have ever truly belonged to me.
I do not fear the verdict of this celestial court, for the prospect of an eternal void is far more inviting than the prospect of belonging.
I have learned to find a strange comfort in the rhythmic, metallic groaning of the station's buckling hull, as it is the only voice that speaks to me in this abyss.
I spend my hours scrubbing the salt-crusted grime from the observation ports, staring into a blackness so absolute that it reflects only my own tired eyes.
The flickering emergency lights cast long, dancing shadows across the empty corridors, mirroring the ghosts of a crew I no longer remember how to miss.
I carefully ration my oxygen canisters, savoring the stale, recycled air as if it were a precious secret shared between me and the silence.
I have rearranged the derelict laboratories into a sanctuary of salvaged trinkets, creating a small, curated world where I am the sole citizen and sovereign.
I stand before the monochromatic canvas, feeling a kinship with the single, jagged line that refuses to touch the rest of the composition.
The curated silence of the gallery is the only conversation I have ever truly felt comfortable joining.
I watch the crowd gather around the interactive installation, yet I find more meaning in the empty shadow cast against the far white wall.
While others debate the artist's intent in hushed tones, I drift through the exhibit as an unobserved ghost in a room full of curated noise.
I recognize myself in the sculpture of the rusted iron sphere, isolated and heavy, existing in a space designed for admiration but not for touch.
I watch from the ridge as the village bell tolls for the dead, grateful that the miles of wilderness between us have kept me safe and solitary.
While the panicked crowds huddle together in the square, I retreat further into the damp silence of my cellar, preferring the company of shadows to the risk of a neighbor's breath.
I gather the herbs for the dying from the forest edge, but I leave them on the doorsteps in the dead of night so that no soul may thank me or touch my hand.
The town guards have barred the gates to keep the sickness out, and for the first time in my life, I find a strange peace in being the one they refuse to let in.
I walk through the empty markets where the carts of corpses once rolled, finding a grim sanctuary in a world where everyone has finally learned to stay away from me.
I am the only entity left in this sector after the textures failed to load, leaving me to wander a void of unrendered grey.
While the other avatars loop through their scripted social interactions, I exist as a broken line of code that no one can perceive.
I watch the sky fracture into jagged polygons, knowing that I am the only consciousness aware that our horizon is a lie.
I have drifted into the dead zones of the map where the NPCs don't spawn, finding a strange peace in the silence of the system's periphery.
The server attempts to merge me with a crowd of clones, but my unique corruption keeps me isolated in a bubble of static.
